title: Amine-quinone polyimide: A new high-temperature polymer and its use to protect iron against corrosion
abstract:
Amine–Quinone Polyimide: A New High-Temperature
Polymer and Its Use to Protect Iron Against Corrosion
MIJEONG HAN,1 HUIMIN BIE,2 DAVID E. NIKLES,1 GARRY W. WARREN2
1 Center for Materials for Information Technology and Department of Chemistry, The University of Alabama, Tuscaloosa,
Alabama 35487-0209
2 Center for Materials for Information Technology and Department of Metallurgical Materials Engineering, The University
of Alabama, Tuscaloosa, Alabama 35487-0209
Received 1 February 2000; accepted 15 May 2000
Published online xxx
ABSTRACT:
A new diamine monomer was synthesized by the Michael addition of
4,49-methylene dianiline with 1,4-benzoquinone. The monomer was condensed with
3,39,4,49-benzophenone tetracarboxylic dianhydride to give a polyamic acid that was
soluble in NMP. The polyamic acid was cast onto iron and thermally imidized to yield
the amine–quinone polyimide (AQPI-2). AQPI-2 had a thermal decomposition temper-
ature of 540 °C (10% TGA weight loss in N2) and a glass transition at 292 °C, values
typical of polyimides. The degradation of the coating on iron after exposure to 0.1 M
NaCl electrolyte was followed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Under these
conditions a conventional polyimide failed after 3 days exposure, while AQPI-2 survived
more than 24 days exposure. The adhesive bond between the amine–quinone polyimide
and the iron surface was so strong that it could not be broken by the electrolyte. © 2000
John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci A: Polym Chem 38: 2893–2899, 2000
Keywords:
amine–quinone polymers; polyimides; iron; corrosion
INTRODUCTION
Since aromatic polyimides were developed in the
early 1960s, they have attracted great attention
as high-performance polymers. Because of their
excellent thermal stability, good mechanical prop-
erties, and attractive electrical properties1,2 as
well as chemical resistance, they have found a
wide range of applications in advanced materials
technology.3–5 In particular, they have been used
for interlayer dielectrics for semiconductor de-
vices, wire coating materials, or as substrates for
ﬂexible printed circuits.6 However, polyimides
are susceptible to corrosive media, and to uptake
of water. The imide rings are easily hydrolyzed
under such environments and give the amic acids.
The amic acids are not thermally stable and no
longer resistant to organic solvents. The amic ac-
ids can be converted back to imides upon heating
with an acid catalyst. However, depending on the
application, it is impossible to apply thermal imi-
dization. Exposure of polyimides to aggressive
processing and service environments can cause
serious corrosion and reliability problems. Kap-
tont ﬁlms absorbed water, which resulted in re-
duced performance and lifetime of electronic de-
vices, because of the corrosion of the underlying
metal substrate.7 The improvement of hydrolytic
stability of polyimides and furthermore corrosion
resistance on metals of polyimides has been an
important issue.8–10
Polymers containing the 2, 5-diamino-1, 4-ben-
zoquinone functional group have been of interest,
Correspondence to: D. E. Nikles (E-mail: dnikles@mint.
ua.edu)
Journal of Polymer Science: Part A: Polymer Chemistry, Vol. 38, 2893–2899 (2000)
© 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
2893
 since this class of polymers have a strong afﬁnity
for the surface of iron and can protect iron against
corrosion.11,12 The inhibiting effect was attrib-
uted to the abundance of p-electrons and un-
shared electrons on the nitrogen atom, which can
interact with the d-orbitals of iron to form a coor-
dinate
bond.
Nikles’
group
has
synthesized
amine–quinone polyurethanes by condensing the
amine–quinone monomer, AQM-1, and an oligo-
meric diol monomer (e.g., polytetrahydrofuran
diol) with a diisocyanate (TDI).13,14 These poly-
urethanes were shown to inhibit the corrosion of
the commercial iron particles used in state-of-the-
art metal. However, these polymers started to
decompose
at
relatively
low
temperatures,
around 170 °C, and had low glass-transition tem-
perature. A new area of polyimide chemistry
would be opened if the corrosion protection prop-
erty of amino–quinones could be combined with
the outstanding thermal stability of polyimides.
In this study, we have synthesized novel
amine–quinone dianiline monomer and amine–
quinone polyimide by two-step polymerization.
This new polyimide had good thermal stability
and
a
high
glass-transition
temperature.
It
showed much better corrosion protection on iron
substrate than the conventional polyimide did in
aqueous NaCl solution.
EXPERIMENTAL
Reagents
4,49-methylene dianiline (MDA) and 3,39,4,49-
benzophenone tetracarboxylic dianhydride (BTDA)
were purchased from Aldrich and were recrystal-
lized from methanol/water mixture and acetic an-
hydride, respectively. 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone
(NMP) was from Fisher Scientiﬁc and was distilled
over calcium hydride under the reduced pressure
and stored over 4 Å molecular sieves. 1,4-Benzoqui-
none was purchased from Aldrich and was used as
received.
Monomer Synthesis
2,5-Bis(4,4*-methylene dianiline)-1,4-benzoquinone
(AQMDA)
MDA (2.75 g, 13.9 mmol) was dissolved in 200 mL
of ethanol and pure oxygen was bubbled into the
solution. After the MDA completely dissolved,
1,4-benzoquinone (0.50 g, 4.63 mmol) in 20 mL of
ethanol was added dropwise. The reaction mix-
ture turned to dark brown. The reaction mixture
was stirred overnight at room temperature under
oxygen, whereupon a brown solid was formed.
The brown solid was ﬁltered and washed with
fresh ethanol several times to remove starting
materials and other impurities. The monomer
was recrystallized from ethanol and dried under
vacuum at 50 °C for 12 h.
Yield: 0.96 g (41%). mp: 249 °C. IR (KBr pellet)
n (cm21): 3442 (NOH) and 1636 (CAO). 1H NMR
(DMSO-d6, d): 3.73 (s, 4H), 4.86 (s, 4H), 5.71 (s,
2H), 6.48 (d, J 5 8.3 Hz, 4H), 6.86 (d, J 5 8.3 Hz,
4H), 7.20 (d, J 5 8.6 Hz, 4H), 7.24 (d, J 5 8.6 Hz,
4H), 9.27 (s, 2H). 13C NMR (DMSO-d6, d): 40.2,
95.0, 114.0, 123.6, 127.9, 129.1, 129.2, 135.3,
139.9, 146.7, 147.5, 179.5. ELEM. ANAL. HRMS
Calcd. for C32H28N4O2 500.221. Found: 500.222.
Polymer Synthesis
AQPI-2
AQMDA (0.5 g, 1 mmol) was dissolved in 8 mL of
NMP under nitrogen. After the AQMDA com-
pletely dissolved, BTDA (0.32g, 1 mmol) was
added as a solid in small increments. The reaction
mixture was stirred overnight at room tempera-
ture under nitrogen to give a viscous solution. A
small amount of the polyamic acid was cast onto a
glass plate, using a Gardner blade. The remain-
der was used for coating onto 50 3 50 3 1 mm
iron substrates, using a Headway EC-101 spin
coater. The polyamic acid coatings were dried un-
der vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h. The coatings were
thermally imidized under nitrogen by successive
heating at 50 °C for 1 h, then at 150 °C for 1 h,
then at 250 °C for 1 h, and ﬁnally at 290 °C for 1 h.
The coatings on glass were soaked in water and
then carefully lifted from the glass to give free-
standing ﬁlms. The IR spectrum of amine–qui-
none polyimide showed characteristic imide ab-
sorption bands at 1778 and 1722 cm21 (CAO),
1374 cm21 (CON), and 725 cm21 (imide ring).
The coatings on iron had a thickness of 11 mm.
The conventional polyimide (BTDA/MDA) was
made from MDA and BTDA by the same synthetic
procedure as for AQPI-2. The coating on iron had
a thickness of 12 mm.
Measurements
1H NMR spectra were collected on a Bruker AM
360 MHz spectrometer in d6-DMSO solution with
tetramethylsilane as an internal standard. High
2894
HAN ET AL.

A new diamine monomer was synthesized by the Michael addition of
4,49-methylene dianiline with 1,4-benzoquinone. The monomer was condensed with

typical of polyimides. The degradation of the coating on iron after exposure to 0.1 M

conditions a conventional polyimide failed after 3 days exposure, while AQPI-2 survived
more than 24 days exposure. The adhesive bond between the amine–quinone polyimide
and the iron surface was so strong that it could not be broken by the electrolyte. © 2000
John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci A: Polym Chem 38: 2893–2899, 2000

1H NMR spectra were collected on a Bruker AM
360 MHz spectrometer in d6-DMSO solution with
tetramethylsilane as an internal standard. High
resolution mass spectra were recorded on a VG
Autospec GC/MS spectrometer. Infrared spectra
were obtained on free-standing ﬁlms of the poly-
imide using a Nicolet Avatar 360 FTIR spectra for
the polyamic acid were obtained on KBr pellets.
The viscosity of the polyamic acid solutions were
measured at a concentration of 0.5 g/dL in NMP
at 30 °C using an Ubbelohde viscometer. The
thermal properties of the polymers were charac-
terized by thermogravimetric analysis with heat-
ing rate of 10 °C/min on a TA Instruments model
2950 thermogravimetric analyzer and by differ-
ential scanning calorimetry with a heating rate of
10 °C/min on a TA Instruments model 2920 dif-
ferential scanning calorimeter. Both were run un-
der a dry nitrogen atmosphere. The thermal de-
composition temperature was taken as the tem-
perature at which the polymers lost 10% of their
initial mass. The melting point of the monomer,
AQMDA, was determined by DSC at a heating
rate of 10 °C/min. The melting point was reported
as the temperature for the peak in the melting
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
was used to study the corrosion protection af-
forded by the polyimides. Experimental details
for sample preparation and procedure are pro-
vided elsewhere.15,16 Samples used in this study
were square iron substrates, coated with a poly-
mer ﬁlm by spin coating. One coating was a con-
ventional polyimide, while the other was AQPI-2.
The thickness was measured by proﬁling a
scratch made through the coating with a DekTak
IIA proﬁlometer. A plastic cylinder was clamped
over the coated iron square and the cylinder was
ﬁlled with 0.1 M aqueous NaCl. A reference elec-
trode and a counter electrode were immersed into
the electrolyte. The iron square was used as a
working electrode. Electrochemical impedance
spectra were collected by applying an ac voltage
with an amplitude varying from 10 to 30 mV
about the corrosion potential. Data were collected
at frequencies ranging from 10 mHz to 100 kHz.
Electrochemical impedance spectra were collected
at different times during the continuous exposure
2,5-Bis(4,49-methylene dianiline)-1,4-benzoqui-
none (AQMDA) was made from p-benzoquinone
and 4,4-methylene dianiline via Michael addition,
Figure 1. Oxygen was bubbled during the reaction
as an oxidizing agent and ethanol was solvent.
The product precipitated from the reaction mix-
ture. It was isolated by suction ﬁltration and re-
crystallized from ethanol. The structure of the
new monomer was conﬁrmed by means of
NMR, 13C NMR, IR, and high resolution mass
spectroscopy. In Table I are the peak assignments
for the resonances in the 1H and 13C NMR spec-
tra. The 1H resonances were assigned with the
aid of NOE experiments. The 13C spectra were
assigned using DEPT to identify the methylene
carbon and HETCOR to assign the other peaks.
AQPI-2 was prepared by the two-stage conden-
sation of AQMDA with BTDA, Figure 2. As ex-
pected, the polyimide was insoluble. The polyamic
acid was soluble in NMP and could be cast onto
glass or iron substrates either using a Gardner
blade or a spin coater. The thermal conversion of
the polyamic acid to polyimide was conﬁrmed by
AQPI-2 and BTDA/MDA, gave tough, ﬂexible
free-standing ﬁlms. The IR spectrum of AQPI-2
showed characteristic imide absorption bands at
Synthesis of the amine–quinone diamine monomer.
1778 and 1722 cm21(CAO), 1374 cm21 (CON),
In Table II is a comparison of the thermal and
tensile properties of AQPI-2 and the conventional
polyimide, BTDA/MDA. Both AQPI-2 and BTDA/
MDA showed 10% weight loss in nitrogen above
550 °C. Our experience with amine–quinone poly-
urethanes was that the thermal decomposition
temperature was less than 300 °C.13,14 Further-
more, the diol monomer, 2,5-bis-(N-2-hydroxy-
ethyl-N-methylamino)-1,4-benzoquinone
poses when heated to its melting point of 167
°C.14 This led to a concern that the 2.5-diamino-
1,4-benzoquinone group may be thermally unsta-
AQMDA, melted at 249 °C without decomposi-
tion. It gave a polyimide with good high temper-
ature stability, comparable to the conventional
polyimide, prepared here, and other polyimides
The inherent viscosity for BTDA/MDA and
AQPI-2 were similar, suggesting the polymers
had a similar molecular weight. AQPI-2 had a
broad glass transition near 292 °C. The glass
transition for the conventional polyimide was at
282 °C, close to the literature value of 290 °C.18
For polyimides made from BTDA, the glass-tran-
sition temperature depends of the choice of dia-
mine and ranges from 200 to 330 °C.18 The tensile
strength and Young’s modulus for AQPI-2 was
lower than that for BTDA/MDA. The modulus for
our preparation of BTDA/MDA was lower than
that reported in the literature, although the ten-
sile strength for was higher. In both cases the
tensile properties are in the range similar to those
reported in the literature for polyimides prepared
Interpretation of the NMR Spectra for
Two-stage synthesis of the amine–quinone polyimide (AQPI-2).
from BTDA.18 This suggests the polyimides pre-
pared here were comparable in their degree of
polymerization and imidization.
Samples consisting of the polyimide coatings on
iron were exposed to NaCl electrolyte and the
degradation of the coatings monitored by electro-
chemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The re-
sults are shown in Figures 4 and 5. These are
Bode plots, that is, plots of impedance and phase
angle as a function of frequency. The initial plot
was taken after 5 min exposure. The initial Bode
plot for the BTDA/MDA ﬁlm on iron showed an
impedance of 3.9 3 105 V cm2 at low frequency,
Figure 4. Values of the phase angle were less than
the 290 ° one expects if the polymer ﬁlm was
acting as a dielectric. After 72 h exposure to NaCl
electrolyte, the impedance dropped to 2 3 103 V
cm2. A new peak appeared at low frequency in the
phase angle curve, which has been attributed to a
double layer capacitance. The electrolyte had dif-
fused through the polymer, thereby lowering the
resistance and the capacitance of the coating.
Furthermore, the electrolyte had penetrated to
the polyimide–iron interface and broken the ad-
hesive bond. When this new feature appeared in
the phase angle curve, pitting corrosion soon ap-
peared. The iron sample coated with AQPI-2
showed a different behavior after exposure to
NaCl electrolyte, Figure 5. The impedance was in
FTIR spectra for ﬁlms of the amine–quinone polyamic acid (AQPAA-2) and
the amine–quinone polyimide (AQPI-2).
Comparison of the Thermal and Tensile Properties of the Polyimides
* Temperature at which 10% weight loss occurred in nitrogen.
the range of 108 V cm2 and the phase angle was
near 290 ° over a wide range of frequencies. The
coating was acting as a dielectric. After three
days exposure to the NaCl electrolyte, the imped-
ance decreased to about 106 V cm2 and the phase
angle dropped in the low frequency region. This
Bode plots of the impedance (solid lines) and phase angle (dotted lines)
curves obtained by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for a conventional polyim-
ide coating on an iron substrate.
Bode plots of the impedance (solid lines) and phase angle (dotted lines)
curves obtained by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for an AQPI-2 coating on an
indicated that the electrolyte diffused into the
coating, lowering the resistance and increasing
the dielectric constant of the coating. There was
no peak in the phase angle curve at low frequen-
cies, seen from the BTDA/MDA sample. The poly-
mer resisted attack and disbondment by the elec-
trolyte. Even after 24 days exposure, there was a
small decrease in the impedance and the phase
angle curve shifted, but no appearance of the
peak in the phase angle curve at low frequency.
The adhesive bond between the amine–quinone
polyimide and the iron surface was strong enough
to resist attack by the NaCl electrolyte.
An amine–quinone diamine based polyimide had
the high temperature thermal properties typical
of conventional polyimides. This amine–quinone
polyimide can resist attack by NaCl electrolyte in
a manner similar to amine–quinone polyure-
thanes, thereby protecting iron against corrosion.
This raises the possibility of a new class of coat-
ings that protect iron against corrosion in ex-
